              From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
              Extinct carnivorous marsupial from Australasia
              "Tarragon" redirects here. For the herb, see Tarragon (Herb). For other uses, see Tarragon (disambiguation).

              Great Tarragon
              Planetary range: Intergalactic (Varied)
              PreꞒ
              Ꞓ

              If you recognize where the image is from, good for you. Don't say so.[1]
              Scientific classification
              Kingdom: Animalia
              Phylum: Chordata
              Class: Reptilia
              Infraclass: Archosauria
              Order: Draconiformes
              Family: †Tarridae
              Genus: †Tarragonus
              Species:
              †Tarragonus Gigantus
              Range map unavailable due to breadth of intergalactic distribution.[4]
              Synonyms
              List
              • Didelphis cynocephala Harris, 1808[3]
              • Dasyurus cynocephalus Geoffroy, 1810[5]
              • Thylacinus harrisii Temminck, 1824[6]
              • Dasyurus lucocephalus Grant, 1831[7]
              • Thylacinus striatus Warlow, 1833[8]
              • Thylacinus communis Anon., 1859[9]
              • Thylacinus breviceps Krefft, 1868[10]
              • Thylacinus rostralis De Vis, 1893[11]

              The Great Tarragon (/ˈθaɪləsiːn/; binomial name Tarragonus Gigantus), also commonly known as the Gigantic Tarragon or simply the Tarragon, is an understudied reptile that has been found across various planets such as Agdaris 8, Zelva 11, and Cryonis 5. Despite their novelty, Tarragons have had very little research performed into their biological nature. They are presumed to be carnivorous, as evidenced by sharp teeth and large claws consistent with those of other carnivores. Some studies have also suggested that they may be omivorous instead, though this is remains hypothetical. Testing and study of most Tarragon species, including the Great Tarragon, is made difficult by the Assembly of Allied Nations' decleration of Tarragon-inhabited planets as uninhabitable, and therefore subject to planetary closure.

              Great Tarragons are the most common species of Tarragon currently known, making up an estimated 70% of the Tarragon population. They are protected by intergalactic law as an understudied species, and the poaching of them is punishable by long prison sentences and immense fines.[12]

              Taxonomy

              Due to a lack of intact Great Tarragon bodies, their taxonomy has remained uncertain since their initial discovery. However, in XXXX AD, PCA herpetologist Petra Whiteheart assembled a taxonomic hypothesis as to Tarragon taxonomy, which has been broadly accepted as a placeholder until more data is acquired.[14]

              Whiteheart's hypothesis generated a new Order, Family, Genus, and set of Species for Tarragons. The Great Tarragon was stated to be of the order Draconiformes, the family Tarridae, the genus Tarragonus, and the speciesTarragonus Gigantus.[16]

              Whiteheart's hypothesis also was the first to propose that Tarragons were omnivores rather than carnivores, though this section of her theory is less widely accepted. Gabriel Moores, an independent American researcher, subsequently released a book titled "The Greatest Misunderstanding", proposing that Tarragons may have been herbivores. However, this theory was rejected by the American Center for Herpetology for the reason that "[Gabriel]'s book, while offering valuable insights in many areas, offers no substantial explanation for the carnivore-like teeth and raptor-like claws of creatures such as the Great Tarragon."[21]

              Evolution

              Due to their multi-planetary distribution, the evolution of most species of Tarragon has been a subject of some debate among the scientific world. Currently, the most widely accepted theory is that Tarragons evolved through convergent evolution, though many scientists believe that they may have been distributed across multiple planets by an unknown alien civilization.[25] They share multiple similarities to other reptiles in the Archosauria infraclass, however do not display traits of any known orders under this. Henry Holdman released a statement in XXXX that he would "dedicate the remainder of [his] life to the study of these enigmatic beasts",[26][27] but was denied adequate funding or permission by the Assembly of Allied Nations. [30]

              The Great Tarragon has been the primary subject of the study of Tarragon evolution, though results have been scarce. The Millard Evolutionary Biology Academy has committed to launching a series of investigations in the year XXXX.[31]

              In order to learn more about Tarragon evolutionary discoveries, refer to Tarragon Evolution.

              Description

              Great Tarragons are estimated to weigh 500-700 tons, although past attempts to place Great Tarragons onto weighing scales were met with unsurprising results.[31] Adult Great Tarragons are typically about 45-50m tall, and measure around 110m long from their snout to the end of their tail.[33] Great Tarragons have membranous wings that make them capable of flight, with a wingspan of around 220m.[28] There do not appear to be any remarkable distinctions in size or weight between male and female Great Tarragons.[34] The bones of Great Tarragons have been found to be hollow, with Tarragons in general relying on their scales to prevent them from being crushed.[36]

              Typically, Great Tarragons are similar to many descriptions of western dragons from mytholigical records.[37] They are covered thoroughly in plate-like scales, visually dissimilar to most reptiles, though these scales are extremely protective,[39] and are composed of the same keratin cells as most reptiles.[40] These scales vary in color but are often bright orange, although they sometimes have black highlights near pointed scale edges along the face and back. [40] Great Tarragon scales have also been observed in shades closer to red and yellow respectively.[41]

              Great Tarragons, like most Tarragons, possess long tails in order to counterbalance their necks and heads,[42] and these tails are typically muscular enough to be utilized as a bludgeoning weapon while fighting.[43] Analysis of its structure yielded inconclusive results as to whether or not the tail is prehensile, though documented behavior or environmental pressures suggesting Great Tarragon tails are prehensile are generally lacking. The tail vertebrae are not fused, resulting in no inherent restriction of tail movement.[40] Tarragon tails, unlike some other lizards, can not be regrown after their loss. The musculature of their tails has been used as an argument for their being predators, and therefore carnivores.[45]

              Unusually for a Tarragon, the Great Tarragon's jaw can only open up to 60 degrees.[46] This appears to be due to it possessing a more compact skull and more rigid scales than most other Tarragons, which can generally open their mouth up to 75 degrees.[48] Great Tarragons also possess fairly long necks, usually accounting for 8-10% of their body length.[49] Unlike many classical interpretations of western dragons, Tarragons have little apparent horn patterning, and very little distinction in horn size or shape between males and females.[50] Tarragon eyes are relatively small compared to the rest of their heads, although eye size and color vary between individual Tarragons.[40]

              Tarragon feet are similar to the feet of other creatures in the infraclass Archosauria. Great Tarragons have no outstanding differences in foot shape compared to other Tarragons besides their size. However, they have large claws lining their feet, which are likely used to cut, grip, and separate meat.[43] The hindfeet are similar to the forefeet but have four digits rather than five. Their claws are non-retractable.[40] Great Tarragons have, on rare occasion, been seen to stand on their hind legs for seconds at a time, though this seems to be used for intimidation.[51]

              Great Tarragons are capable of flight, though the circumstances in which they fly often results in a predictable difficulty in collecting data, making it unclear what their maximum speeds are. They have been observed in sustained flight for only short periods of time, however, usually between 2-8 minutes.[40] In her hypothesis, Petra Whiteheart suggests that their wings may be partly for display to potential mates, though any form of mating ritual or display has yet to be documented.[41]

              Observers of the animal in the wild noted that it would growl and hiss when agitated, sometimes accompanied by a roar.[53] While mated pairs of Great Tarragons have been observed, there has been little success in finding fresh pairs that have not yet fully bonded, nor any Great Tarragons actively seeking a mate. However, their mating season appears to be spring.[54]

              Distribution and habitat

              Great Tarragons are the most common species of Tarragon, making up approximately 70% of all Tarragons universally. Great Tarragons are most commonly found on Agdaris 8, making up around 94% of Agradian Tarragons. They have also been observed on Zelva 11 in large numbers, though other environmental factors make them less common on this planet.[43] In general, Great Tarragons reside in plains, mountains, hills, and sparse forests. Depending on the planet, which land masses they are found in varies, though they are consistently found in the largest land masses. Great Tarragons also appear to be highly temperature-resilient, but prefer temperate climates and conditions. They have been observed using their wing membranes as a form of protection from the rain.[55]

              On Agdaris 8, they have shown interest in residing near deposits of Polara, an ore found close to the surface of the planet.[59] Great Tarragons have no apparent camouflage strategy, relying on their size and speed to keep themselves well-protected and successful hunters.[40] Great Tarragons have a typical roaming distance of 60-70km.2 (37-43 sq mi),[41] and they have been observed to be relatively territorial creatures. Males tend toward more territorial behavior.[61]

              Ecology and behaviour

              Reproduction

              There is little evidence for year-round breeding among Great Tarragons, with mating season seeming to be in Spring. However, eggs have been found at times that indicate mating outside of Spring.[40] Great Tarragons typically lay 3-4 eggs. The eggs are white with brown spots, though yellow-scaled Great Tarragons sometimes have grayer spots on the egg shells.[62] Great Tarragons attempt to find a new nest for each breeding attempt, but may return to old nests if a sufficient location is not found.[40]

              There is very little data concerning the behavior of Great Tarragons seeking mates. It has been proposed that they may use their wings in a manner of display ritual, but this information has not yet been verified.[65] No Great Tarragons have been successfully placed into captivity before, leading to further difficulty in studying their behavior. Regardless of this, it is presumed that both males and females exhibit mostly traditional signs of seeking a mate. [66]

              Feeding and diet

              Great Tarragons are presumed to be carnivorous apex predators and crepuscular hunters, spending their waking hours mostly roaming and protecting their territory. [67]

              There have been alternative propositions as to what Great Tarragon diets consist of, including Petra Whiteheart's proposition that they are omnivores, and Gabriel Moores' proposition that they were herbivores. Moores' hypothesis is largely rejected due to the presence of sharp, carnivore-likefangs lining the mouths of Great Tarragons. The concept of omnivorous Tarragons is supported by the fact that Blue-Tipped Tarragons, a smaller species endemic to Cryonis 5, are omnivores by the necessity of their environment. However, there have been few reports of Tarragons eating.

              In the two instances of successful Great Tarragon killings, the contents of the stomach have been completely evicerated, once though Orbital Laser strikes and once through the use of DA-5 Biological Disintegration Weapons.[89]

              Research

              Research into Great Tarragons relies heavily on AAN approval, which is somewhat difficult to acquire. The Millard Evolutionary Biology Academy has acquired exclusive authorization to lead research expeditions through Agdaris 8 and Zelva 11 in order to document them further.[134] Much of the current research was conducted through natural observation, as direct interaction with the Tarragons has resulted in the destruction of multiple ACs and other large-scale research craft in the past.[135]

              Cultural significance

              In popular culture

              The Great Tarragon is the most well-known Tarragon species by the general public. Upon its initial discovery, several social media sites reported outages having to do with content influxes.[156] Although their novelty has worn since their discovery, Great Tarragons remain a well-known symbol of fantasy. However, the Planetary Closure Administration's action to close any planets containing Great Tarragons has sparked some civil protests throughout the years.[156]

              According to The Daily Reader, Great Tarragons have become a pop culture idol representing fantasy and the idea that "anything is possible".[161]

              Augmentation Surgury

              According to a XXXX study by Johns Hopkins Augmentation Center, approximately 39% of augmented humans undergoing surgery to receive features resembling Western dragons described their surgery as giving them Tarragon-like features rather than Dragon-like features.[163] Several augmentation surgeons have reported patients requesting Tarragon-like augmentations. However, only 16% of augmented humans who received dragon-like augmentation surgery then said their augmentations were Tarragon-like retroactively.[164]

              In Eastern Culture

              According to the same study conducted by the JHAC, Great Tarragons were less commonly known in traditionally Eastern regions or planets. This is likely due to their similarity to Western dragons rather than their Eastern counterparts.[176]

              See also

              • Paddle, Robert (2000). The Last Tasmanian Tiger: the History and Extinction of the Thylacine. Cambridge University Press. ISBN 978-0-521-53154-2.

              Further reading

              • Bailey, Col (2013). Shadow of the Thylacine: One Man's Epic Search for the Tasmanian Tiger. Scoresby, Vic.: Five Mile Press. ISBN 978-1-74346-485-4.
              • Freeman, Carol (2010). Paper Tiger: A Visual History of the Thylacine. Human-animal studies. Leiden ; Boston: Brill. ISBN 978-90-04-18165-6. OCLC 643081588.
              • Guiler, Eric R. (1985). Thylacine: The Tragedy of the Tasmanian Tiger. Melbourne: Oxford University Press. ISBN 978-0-19-554603-3.
              • ——; Godard, Philippe; Maguire, David (1998). Tasmanian Tiger: A Lesson to be Learnt. Perth, W.A: Abrolhos Publishng. ISBN 978-0-9585791-0-0.
              • Guiler, Eric R. (1961a). "Breeding Season of the Thylacine". Journal of Mammalogy. 42 (3): 396–397. doi:10.2307/1377040. JSTOR 1377040.
              • Guiler, E. R. (1961b). "The former distribution and decline of the Thylacine". Australian Journal of Science. 23 (7): 207–210.
              • Heath, Alan (2014). Thylacine: Confirming Tasmanian Tigers Still Live. Chicago: Vivid Publishing. ISBN 978-1-925209-40-2.
              • Leigh, Julia (1999). The Hunter. Ringwood, Australia: Penguin Books Australia. ISBN 978-0-140-28351-8.
              • Lord, Clive Errol (1927). "Existing Tasmanian marsupials". Papers and Proceedings of the Royal Society of Tasmania. 61. Royal Society of Tasmania: 17–24. doi:10.26749/GTOS1305. ISSN 0080-4703.
              • Lowry, David C.; Lowry, Jacoba W. J. (January 1967). "Discovery of a Thylacine (Tasmanian Tiger) Carcase In a Cave Near Eucla, Western Australia". Helictite. 5 (2): 25–29.
              • Pearce, R. (1976). "Thylacines in Tasmania". Australian Mammal Society Bulletin. 3: 58.
              • S., Sleightholme; N., Ayliffe (2005). International Thylacine Specimen Database (CD-ROM) (Master Copy ed.). London: Zoological Society.
              • Smith, Steven J. (1981). The Tasmanian Tiger – 1980. A report on an investigation of the current status of thylacine Thylacinus cynocephalus, funded by the World Wildlife Fund Australia. Wildlife Division technical report. Hobart, Tasmania: National Parks and Wildlife Service. ISBN 978-0-724-61753-1.
              • Heinz F. Moeller: Der Beutelwolf. Thylacinus cynocephalus. Westarp-Wissenschaften, Magdeburg 1997, ISBN 3-89432-869-X (Die Neue Brehm-Bücherei, Band 642).
              • Holmes, B., & Linnard, G. (Eds.). (2023). Thylacine: The History, Ecology and Loss of the Tasmanian Tiger. CSIRO PUBLISHING. ISBN 9781486315536
